q: Most of the fluids are ideal ones and offer no resistance to motion.
Viscosity arises when there is relative motion between layers of the fluid.
A fluid with large viscosity resists motion because its strong intermolecular forces give it a lot of internal friction.
A fluid with low viscosity flows with great difficulty because its molecular make up results in very high friction.
The viscosity of liquids increases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases increases with an increase in temperature.
Viscosity is an extensive property because it changes as the amount of matter changes.
The SI unit of viscosity is pascal second ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$).
The unit symbol $\text{N}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}$ stands for newton second per metre squared.
Kinematic viscosity is used to measure fluid's resistance to flow when an external force is applied.
Dynamic viscosity is also known as absolute viscosity.
Kinematic viscosity is more useful than dynamic viscosity to measure the resistive flow of a fluid under the weight of gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states that shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient.
In symbols, shear stress is represented by the Greek letter $\eta$.
Shear stress is the shearing force per unit volume.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the velocity gradient.
The formula for volume flow rate used in the continuity equation examples is $\frac{V}{t} = Av$.
The term $\frac{dv}{dy}$ in Newton's law of viscosity represents the pressure gradient.
In the equation $\tau = -\eta \frac{dv}{dy}$, the negative sign shows the direction of the shearing force.
Aviation is an application of fluid dynamics that deals with motion underneath the surface of water.
The density of air remains constant in the room heating duct problem of Example 3.2.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of velocity gradient to the shearing stress.
Stokes' law expresses the drag force resisting the fall of small spherical objects through a fluid medium under the influence of gravity.
The drag force acting upward in resistance to the fall of a spherical object in a viscous liquid increases as the velocity becomes constant at terminal velocity.
In the formula for the weight of a sphere ball, $w = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \rho_1 g$, the variable $\rho_1$ is the density of the liquid.
At a constant velocity of fall, the upward and downward forces acting on the spherical object are in balance.
